title: General Applications of Each Method
narrationScript: Knowing how each method works is half the job. Knowing where each one belongs is the other half, and that is what this lesson is for. Method selection is a system decision. The inputs are the specification and the sensitivity level it demands, the part itself, its material, its surface condition, its geometry, how many of them are coming at you, and the place where the work happens, with whatever water, power, and lighting that place can offer. Your written procedure makes the final call. Your job is to understand why it calls what it calls, and to recognize when a job does not fit the procedure in front of you. Start with water washable, Method A. It earns its keep on volume and on difficult surfaces. Picture baskets of aluminum castings coming off a line, hundreds per shift, with as cast surfaces and cored holes. Nobody is hand wiping those. A water washable fluorescent line rinses penetrant cleanly out of threads, keyways, drilled holes, and rough surfaces, and it does it fast. The price is the over wash risk we covered in the last lesson, so shallow, broad flaws like forging laps are the ones to worry about. When a specification is serious about shallow flaws, it often pushes the work up to a post emulsifiable method. That is where Methods B and D live, on fatigue critical, finished machined hardware. Turbine engine blades and disks. Landing gear. Shafts and fasteners. Parts where the flaw that matters is a tight fatigue crack and the spec calls for sensitivity level three or four. Hydrophilic processing, Method D, dominates that work today because every step of it can be measured and controlled, the pre rinse, the remover concentration, the contact time, the final rinse. Lipophilic lines, Method B, still run in some shops, and the selection logic is the same. Post emulsifiable methods protect shallow entrapments during removal, and they charge you for that protection in process control work. Method C, solvent removable, is the field and maintenance method. One girth weld on a pipeline right of way. A nozzle weld up in a pipe rack. A suspect area found during an outage, a long way from any tank line. With a visible red dye kit you need three aerosol cans, some lint free wipes, and at least about one hundred footcandles of white light at the surface, which daylight or a decent work light will give you. No water, no mains power, no darkened booth. That portability is why Method C with Type two penetrant is the default answer for localized field work. The same removal method also runs with fluorescent penetrant for small high sensitivity checks in the shop, but then you owe the lamp its conditions, and that brings us to lighting. Fluorescent examination needs an ultraviolet intensity of at least one thousand microwatts per square centimeter at the surface, with ambient white light held to about two footcandles or less. That means a darkened booth or a blacked out enclosure, plus a few minutes for your eyes to adapt before you evaluate anything. Try to arrange that on open structural steel at noon and you will understand why field work defaults to visible dye. Temperature is the other environmental gate. The standard processing band in most procedures runs from about forty to one hundred twenty five degrees Fahrenheit. A part out in winter cold or sitting near hot process equipment is outside the band, and that calls for a procedure qualified for the actual temperature, longer dwells at the cool end, special high temperature materials at the hot end, not improvisation. One more selection factor outranks convenience, and that is chemistry. Nickel alloys, austenitic stainless steels, and titanium take low sulfur, low halogen materials, because residues left in a flaw can attack those alloys in service. Oxygen system hardware takes specially compatible penetrant materials, never the standard shop cans. When a new job lands, run the checklist in order. What does the spec demand. What is the part, and what is its surface. How many are there. Where is the work, and what can I control there. Answer those honestly and the method usually picks itself. Then confirm the answer against the written procedure before anything sprays, because the procedure, not the habit, is what you are qualified to.
imagePrompt: A technician kneels beside an excavated pipeline girth weld outdoors, spraying red visible penetrant from an aerosol can onto the weld, with solvent wipes and a white developer can staged on a drop cloth nearby.

sections.heading: Selection Is a System Decision
sections.body:
No penetrant method is best. Each is best *for something*, and matching them is a system decision with five inputs:

- **The specification** - which practice is invoked, which type, and what sensitivity level the flaw of concern demands.
- **The part** - material and chemistry limits (nickel alloys, austenitic stainless steels, and titanium take low-sulfur, low-halogen materials; oxygen-service hardware takes LOX-compatible penetrants), plus geometry: threads, keyways, drilled and cored holes.
- **The surface** - smooth machined faces wipe and emulsify predictably; rough as-cast and forged skins hold penetrant and demand easy rinsing.
- **The quantity** - six hundred castings a shift is a different problem from one weld.
- **The place** - a shop line with tanks, dryer, and a darkened UV booth, or a pipe rack with neither water nor power; and the **temperature band**, typically about 40–125 °F for standard processing.

The written procedure makes the final call - your job is to understand the call and to flag the job that does not fit it. The sections below walk each method through its natural territory; the decision flow then puts it together.

sections.heading: Where Method A Earns Its Keep
sections.body:
Method A's territory is **volume plus difficult surfaces**. Think baskets of aluminum or magnesium sand castings, steel forgings, production weldments - hundreds per shift, processed on a fixed fluorescent line: penetrant dip, drain, controlled wash, dryer, dry-powder developer, UV booth. The water spray rinses cleanly from **threads, keyways, cored passages, and rough skins** that hand-wiping could never clear, which is exactly why the high-volume world runs water-washable.

Its boundaries are equally clear. The over-wash risk means **shallow, broad discontinuities** - forging laps, smeared-over cracks - are the flaws most likely to be lost, so when a specification worries about those, it tends to push the work to a post-emulsifiable method or to tighten wash controls. And on safety-critical hardware where the flaw of concern is a tight fatigue crack at sensitivity level 3 or 4, Method A is usually not offered at all.

Type pairing: most production Method A lines run Type I fluorescent for sensitivity and scanning speed; Type II water-washable serves less critical, well-lit work such as weldment checks where a booth is unavailable but a wash station is.

sections.heading: Where B and D Earn Theirs
sections.body:
Post-emulsifiable methods live on **fatigue-critical, finished-machined hardware**: turbine blades and disks, landing-gear components, shafts, fasteners - parts where the customer's flaw of concern is a tight, shallow fatigue crack and the specification demands **sensitivity level 3 or 4** under an aerospace practice.

**Method D (hydrophilic)** dominates this work on modern lines because every step is measurable: water pre-rinse, remover concentration verified by refractometer, timed contact, final rinse, each logged. **Method B (lipophilic)** still runs on legacy lines and in procedures that retain it; the selection logic is the same, with contact-time discipline as the price of admission.

Two application cautions. First, post-emulsifiable removal is **less forgiving on very rough surfaces and tangled geometry** - emulsifier and remover are hard to apply and rinse uniformly from deep recesses, which is part of why rough castings stay with Method A. Second, on machined aerospace hardware, prior operations such as **peening, abrasive blasting, or aggressive machining can smear metal over cracks**; such parts are commonly **etched before penetrant testing** per the governing specification, whatever method is used - method selection does not cure a flaw that has been mechanically sealed shut.

sections.caption: A field decision flow for method selection - utilities and viewing conditions first, then sensitivity demand, then volume and surface condition.

sections.heading: Method C: The Field and Maintenance Method
sections.body:
Method C answers the jobs that cannot come to a tank line. A girth weld on a pipeline right-of-way. A nozzle weld in a refinery pipe rack during a turnaround. A suspect area found on in-service equipment. The classic kit is **Type II visible dye**: three aerosol cans - cleaner/remover, red penetrant, nonaqueous developer - plus lint-free wipes and a light meter. The requirements travel with it: at least about **100 fc (≈1,000 lux) of white light at the examination surface** (daylight or a work light - verified with the meter, not guessed), surface temperature inside the standard band, and the wipe-don't-flood removal discipline.

**Type I fluorescent, solvent-removable** covers small-area, high-sensitivity rechecks - but fluorescent viewing conditions travel poorly: the lamp owes you **UV-A ≥ 1,000 µW/cm² at the surface with ambient white light ≤ 2 fc**, which in the field means blackout shrouds and dark-adaptation time.

The economic boundary: wiping is slow, so Method C makes sense on **localized examinations** - a weld, a repair excavation, a bearing seat - not broad surfaces. The moment the job becomes "the whole vessel," it belongs on a processing line or a different schedule.

sections.caption: General applications - the typical territory of each method.
rows: A - water-washable
High-volume castings, forgings, production weldments
Rough as-cast or forged skins; threads, keyways, cored holes
Fixed fluorescent line: tanks, dryer, UV booth
Over-wash on shallow, broad flaws such as laps
B - post-emulsifiable lipophilic
Legacy high-sensitivity lines; machined hardware
Smooth machined surfaces
Immersion line with timed emulsifier station
Contact-time drift; agitation during contact
C - solvent-removable
Field welds, maintenance and in-service checks, localized exams
Smooth to moderately rough; small areas only
Anywhere - no water or power; verified white light
Flooding solvent; stretching the kit over large areas
D - post-emulsifiable hydrophilic
Aerospace engine and structural hardware at level 3–4
Finished machined surfaces
Controlled hydrophilic line with darkened UV booth
Remover concentration and contact-time control

sections.title: Field chemistry is still chemistry
sections.body: Solvent removers and aerosol penetrants in the field demand **ventilation** (a confined-space weld check builds vapor fast), **flammability control** (keep cans and soaked wipes away from hot work and ignition sources), gloves (solvents defat skin), and proper disposal of spent cans and wipes. One absolute: **oxygen-service hardware takes LOX-compatible penetrant materials only** - residues from standard shop materials can react violently in oxygen service.

sections.caption: Two working environments: the fixed shop processing line for Methods A, B, and D versus the portable Method C aerosol kit.

sections.heading: Lighting and Environment Close the Loop
sections.body:
Two environmental gates finish most method-selection arguments.

**Lighting.** Type I fluorescent examination requires **UV-A intensity of at least 1,000 µW/cm² at the examination surface** with **ambient white light held to about 2 fc (≈20 lux) or less**, plus several minutes of eye dark-adaptation - conditions a booth provides effortlessly and an open structure at noon cannot, short of full blackout. Type II requires **at least about 100 fc (≈1,000 lux) of white light at the surface**, achievable nearly anywhere with a work light and a meter. That asymmetry, more than raw sensitivity, is why field work defaults to visible dye while shop lines default to fluorescent.

**Temperature.** Standard processing bands run about **40–125 °F**. Cold metal slows penetrant action - procedures typically extend dwell at the cool end - and parts outside the band need a procedure qualified for the actual temperature (high-temperature penetrant families exist for hot work). Winter pipe steel at 25 °F is not a judgment call; it is a procedure question.

Spec, part, surface, quantity, place: answer them in order and the method usually picks itself - then the written procedure confirms it.

figures.title: Fixed processing line versus field kit

interactive.title: The Method Call
interactive.intro: Four jobs land on your bench this week. For each one, make the call a Level II should make - then check the reasoning. In every case the written procedure has the final word; your job is to know what it should say and why.

steps.prompt: Job 1: a single girth weld on a pipeline section at a remote right-of-way. No water supply, no mains power, full daylight. The contract requires penetrant testing of the weld and heat-affected zone.
options: Type I fluorescent, Method A - truck in water and rig a portable wash station
Type II visible, Method C - aerosol kit with verified white light at the surface
Type I fluorescent, Method D - highest sensitivity available, so it must be the safest call
steps.feedback: Method C with visible dye exists for exactly this job: a localized examination with no utilities and no darkened area. Verify at least about 100 fc of white light at the surface, wipe with dry then solvent-dampened cloths, and never flood the solvent. Fluorescent methods die in daylight - you cannot hold ambient light to about 2 fc on an open right-of-way.

steps.prompt: Job 2: six hundred small aluminum sand castings per shift, as-cast surfaces with cored holes, processed in baskets on a fluorescent line. The specification requires sensitivity level 2.
options: Method A water-washable with dry-powder developer - controlled wash, stop at background
Method C solvent-removable - wipe each casting individually for maximum control
Method D hydrophilic - more process control is always the better choice
steps.feedback: Volume plus rough surfaces plus a modest sensitivity level is water-washable territory: the spray rinses cored holes and rough skins no cloth could clear, at a cycle time hand methods cannot touch. The discipline that keeps it honest is the wash itself - coarse spray, commonly no more than about 40 psi at roughly 50–100 °F, stopping at background under UV-A.

steps.prompt: Job 3: finished-machined compressor blades in for engine overhaul. The purchase order invokes the aerospace practice and requires Type I, sensitivity level 4.
options: Method A - the shop's water-washable line is already running and throughput is excellent
Method D hydrophilic post-emulsifiable - pre-rinse, concentration-checked remover, timed contact, final rinse
Method C visible-dye bench check - quicker than running the line
steps.feedback: Level 4 on fatigue-critical machined hardware is hydrophilic post-emulsifiable territory: the detergent remover acts only at the surface, so tight, shallow fatigue cracks keep their entrapment through removal. Expect QPL-listed materials, refractometer checks on remover concentration, and timed contact. A water wash risks stripping exactly the flaw you were hired to find - and visible dye cannot reach level 4 at all.

steps.prompt: Job 4: your Method A line keeps losing a known shallow forging lap on the customer's reference part, even with the wash run carefully to procedure. Fluorescent examination is still required. What is the right move?
options: Extend the wash until the background is perfect - a cleaner surface means a more sensitive test
Propose, through your Level III, requalifying the job to a post-emulsifiable method, which protects shallow entrapments during removal
Quietly skip the wash step on that part so the lap cannot wash out
steps.feedback: Shallow, broad flaws are the classic over-wash casualties, and when careful Method A processing still loses them, the method - not the operator - is the limit. Post-emulsifiable removal acts only on the surface layer, which is exactly the protection a shallow lap needs. Method changes go through the Level III and the procedure, never through quiet improvisation - and skipping the wash would bury every indication in background.

checkQuestions.question: A production lot of steel forgings has threaded bosses, keyways, and rough skins - five hundred pieces per shift. Which removal reality most favors Method A here?
options: Water spray rinses penetrant cleanly from recesses and rough surfaces that hand-wiping cannot clear
Water-washable penetrant is the most sensitive family available
Method A requires no process controls, so the line runs faster
Solvent wiping is faster than spraying on threaded parts
checkQuestions.explanation: Method A's case is throughput and geometry: a controlled spray reaches threads, keyways, and rough skins that no cloth could clear - while its sensitivity is good, not best, and its wash still needs real control.

checkQuestions.question: What most fundamentally pushes outdoor, in-place weld examinations toward Type II visible penetrant?
options: Red dye penetrates deeper into flaws than fluorescent penetrant
Fluorescent viewing needs ambient white light held to about 2 fc, which is impractical outdoors without full blackout
Visible penetrant requires no removal step
UV-A lamps are prohibited outside of shops
checkQuestions.explanation: Type II needs only about 100 fc of white light at the surface, which daylight or a work light provides; Type I demands a near-dark surface plus UV-A, which an open structure cannot offer without blackout enclosures.

checkQuestions.question: On a hydrophilic (Method D) line, the plain-water pre-rinse before the remover bath serves to:
options: Pre-develop the indications for an early look
Warm the part into the processing temperature band
Remove the bulk of the surface penetrant so the remover does less work and the bath lasts longer
Neutralize the penetrant dye before removal
checkQuestions.explanation: The pre-rinse strips most of the surface excess before the detergent stage, reducing the load on the remover bath and keeping the controlled removal step short and uniform.

checkQuestions.question: A pipe weld must be tested in a yard where the steel is at 30 °F. Your procedure's standard band is 40–125 °F. The correct action is:
options: Proceed - penetrant always works on clean steel
Halve the dwell time to limit the cold exposure
Switch to a higher sensitivity level to compensate for the cold
Stop - use a procedure qualified for the actual temperature, or bring the surface into the band per procedure
checkQuestions.explanation: Outside the standard band the process is no longer qualified: cold thickens penetrant and slows capillary entry. The answer is a qualified low-temperature technique or warming the part as the procedure allows - never improvisation.

checkQuestions.question: PT is requested on titanium and austenitic stainless steel components. Which materials consideration applies?
options: Any penetrant family may be used as long as it is fresh
Use low-sulfur, low-halogen penetrant materials with residue limits controlled per analysis
Only visible dye may be used on titanium
Fluorescent penetrants are prohibited on stainless steel
checkQuestions.explanation: Sulfur and halogen residues left in flaws can attack nickel alloys, austenitic stainless steels, and titanium in service, so these alloys take low-sulfur, low-halogen formulations with contaminant limits certified by analysis.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §3.3 (general applications of each method)
ASTM E165/E165M and ASTM E1417/E1417M, application and processing requirements (cited generically)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 6 - code work lighting and processing requirements (cited generically)
SAE AMS 2644 - sensitivity levels and material classification (cited generically)
Generic materials-compatibility practice: low-sulfur/low-halogen formulations and LOX-compatible penetrants per governing specifications
